headline: Haiti's Youth Rise Up Against Corruption With New Training
meta_description: Haiti empowers young leaders through anti-corruption training, building a long-term strategy to strengthen institutions and fight impunity.
summary_short: Haiti's government and UN are investing in youth to tackle corruption through specialized training, integrity clubs, and strengthened judicial units.
body:
In a country where more than half the population is under 25, Haiti is placing its faith in young people to reshape the future. The Government and United Nations are betting that training the next generation in integrity and accountability can help dismantle corruption entrenched in weak institutions and widespread impunity.

Last summer, 60 students and young civil society leaders—selected from 180 applicants—attended Haiti's first Summer School on Anti-Corruption, organized by the Government's Anti-Corruption Unit with UN support. Through intensive workshops and real-life scenarios, participants learned to identify, prevent, and report corruption, gaining hands-on experience with official reporting mechanisms like the anti-corruption hotline (5656).

The impact was immediate. One participant, Adamson Junior Lavéus, described how the programme helped him understand corruption's many forms—extortion, illicit enrichment, misappropriation of public assets—and reinforced that civic responsibility belongs to everyone. The success sparked such interest that a second Summer School attracted hundreds of applications within days.

Beyond summer programmes, around 50 integrity clubs now operate in schools across Haiti, where students develop a genuine culture of integrity through role-playing and awareness campaigns. The strategy also includes two newly inaugurated specialized judicial units staffed by 25 trained magistrates to investigate financial crime and mass crimes.

Adrian Banu, UNODC's anti-corruption adviser, views this work as a 20-year investment. Drawing on his experience in Romania—where corruption decreased significantly over three decades—he remains optimistic: progress is possible, even in Haiti.
